speaker: interviewer
text: --Bitton, tape 5. Ike, where did you land when you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We flew from New York to Milwaukee, Wisconsin. And we had the wife of my wife's cousin, Ann [NON-ENGLISH], come to pick us up from the airport in Milwaukee. And she drove us to Oshkosh, Wisconsin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had a very nice welcome. They had-- they helped us, obviously, with renting a home for us. And I started to work about a week later. I started to work at Plywood Oshkosh. And we work sorting all kinds of plywood, which I didn't know nothing about at the time. And I learned quite a bit. And I started, then, to sell plywood. I'm a very good salesman, I must say. Because I care to, to-- for the product, the quality, and the customer. And after a short period of time, the assistant manager left in Madison, Wisconsin. And so I was sent in to fill that position as assistant manager for Applied Madison again, selling plywood. And after a short period of time, I was giving the opportunity to go out on the field and solicit business. So not knowing exactly what to do, I went to the public library in Madison, Wisconsin. And I looked at the Dun and Bradstreet. And I selected five companies that did business, that they were using plywood. And one was Marshall Erdman and Associates. And they were all in the medical field. And, and the other one was the Professional Office Buildings. Professional Office Buildings, I knocked at the door. I decided never to call on a businessman on a Monday. Better call him on a Wednesday, middle of the week, never in the morning, right after lunch, after the, the executive has his belly full and maybe a, a little drink of sorts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Professional Office Buildings and I was advised that they didn't have any needs, and, and I should leave my card. It was one of the engineers that I talked to. So I said, listen, sir, I appreciate your offer, but is the owner in? He said yes, Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. Well, let me talk to him, please. So he let me in. Mr. [INAUDIBLE] sat by his desk with a big cigar. And he said, how come no one from your company ever came and called me, and I am here several years? And I thought for a moment and said-- well, this guy's a tough cookie, I thought to myself. I said, I have to give him the right answer otherwise he's gonna kick me out. So I said, sir, if you ask me why I am here, I can answer that question intelligently. If you ask me why nobody came to see you before, I cannot answer that intelligently. Then he said, sit down. And from there on, I sold him a lot of cabinets and plywood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And ultimately, he gave me a job. I went to work for him. And we went to Europe. I was a consultant. We went to Germany. We went to Italy, to England, because I speak different languages, and I was able to help him in, in other areas as well. But after a given period of time, I had the opportunity to come to Woodstock, Illinois, because our cousin was building a, a store in here. So I asked him if I could take over the store. He gave me two options-- Woodstock, Illinois or Champaign-Urbana. So I told him, listen, give me Woodstock because it's close to Madison, and I can commute between Madison and Woodstock, at least for the-- at the beginning. So that's how come I arrived in Woodstock, Illinois. And I'm still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you own your own business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you kept your ties with Portugal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. I represent the Jewish community of Portugal in the United States, at least in one particular area, which is the, the authority to solicit funds for the renovation and restoration of an old Jewish cemetery in Faro, Portugal, which I was able to do very successfully in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has that cemetery been refurbished and redone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the cemetery was, was restored to, to-- dignity was restored to God and to the 106 souls which are buried there, because it was pretty much, pretty much semi-abandoned since 19-- 1940s. There is no Jewish community in Faro, other than the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you done anything to document all of these experiences that you have had, Ike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've been working on a manuscript now for several years. The manuscript is pretty much complete now with the multitude of these stories. And I hope to be able to publish it soon. And by the way, in Faro, I was very, very fortunate to find a gentleman from South Africa, a very religious man by the name of Ralf Pinto, who offered his services to act on, on our behalf, on my behalf. And I, I nominated him director of operations. And he's doing so, and he's doing a fabulous job in Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm very fortunate to have had this opportunity of restoring a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Beth Shalom, the house of the world that was really very, very neglected, simply because there was no community. And the nearest community is the community of the Jews in Lisbon, which is about three and a half hours away. And who is going to drive three and a half hours just to visit or take care of a cemetery? So now we do have a, a person that takes care of a cemetery, other than Mr. Ralf Pinto. And we hope to be able to continue to find donors to help us in maintaining this Beth Shalom. Ike, how do you feel that telling of your experiences during the war and after the war can help others?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sincerely would hope that the time that we have spent here now in videotaping these testimonials would no go to waste. And those that see this would-- I would hope they would understand [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how some of these great injustice were committed against Jewish people simply because they are Jews and try to prevent this from happening again, not just to Jews but to anybody else, to any other ethnic group in the world. That I hope for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Portuguese consul Dr. Aristides de Sousa Mendes with Rabbi Kruger. I believe it was taken at the residence of Dr. de Sousa Mendes in the north of Portugal in Cabanas de Viriato. And it has to be sometime in 1945 or '46, perhaps after the war. This picture shows my late Aunt Esther holding the box of matzot during the Passover of 1944 at the Lisbon soup kitchen in Portugal with a group of Holocaust refugees. This picture was taken at the Port of Lisbon. It's of a group of children from the Holocaust at the Gare de Obidos prior to boarding the for Eretz, Israel in 1944. This is a picture taken aboard the on the way to Eretz, Israel, Palestine in 1944, January 23rd. And it's my brother, Abraham, God bless his soul, myself, and two groups of refugees, one from Poland and one from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you and which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, I am on the left. And next to me is-- to the right is Abraham. This is a picture taken also at the Port of Lisbon. And on the right is Yoel Sequerra with a group of refugees, children from the Holocaust. And it was taken in 1944 also. This is another picture taken on January 23rd, 1944 at the Port of Lisbon prior to the boarding of the And these are all children of the Holocaust. This picture was taken in 1946 upon my discharge from the Jewish Brigade Group. This is a picture taken in Lisbon at the residence of my sister Hanita with my father, God bless his soul, David Bitton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- it was taken in 1955. This is a picture taken in 1972 in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe, Madison, Wisconsin. And it consists of Miriam, my wife, the first one from the left, next, her mother, Dorotea David, next, my daughter Tamara and her daughter, Nadia. This picture was taken in Lisbon at a community, Jewish community center in 1972, and it consists of from the left to the right my brother Jacob, myself, my brother Messod, now deceased, and my brother Joseph, my sister Tamo, from the left, my sister Hanita in the center, and my sister Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I said it was 1970-- '76. Did I say that? This picture was taken in Washington, DC in 1987 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the occasion of the presentation by the president of Portugal with the Medal of Liberty posthumously to the family of Dr. Aristides de Sousa Mendes. In this picture, from the left to the right, we see Carlos, his son, his daughter Joana, his son Sebastian, myself, whom they used to call Brother Isaac, and then comes John Abranches, and Philippe, which is now deceased. This picture was taken in Chicago on Independence Day, 1986. And it consists of myself and my wife, Miriam. This picture was taken in Zurich, Switzerland, 1994. And it consists of from the right to the left, my daughter Tamara, her husband, Ernst Loosli, her daughter Tina, and her daughter Nadia, and her son, Robi. This is a picture of both our sons, from the left, Daniel and Michael, taken in 1989 at the Galt Airport near Woodstock, Illinois. This is a picture of our son Michael with his wife, Trish, and their sons from the right, David and left, Joey, taken in Woodstock, Illinois in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to take this opportunity and introduce my wife, Miriam. We have been married now-- it's going to be half a century, 50 years on July 3rd, 1997. And I do not know how she kept up with me all these years. But I'm, I'm pleased that we are together, and we have raised three beautiful children and seven, seven grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one great-grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's right, one great-grandchild. So I, I also want to take this opportunity and to thank, thank you, the, the Steven Spielberg HaShoah Foundation, Survivors of the Holocaust, for have given us the opportunity to listen to my testimonial about what I had seen concerning the, not just the refugees, the survivors of the HaShoah in Portugal, in Israel, in Belgium, Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I would hope that this testimony will really show others what had transpired and hopefully that it does not happen again to anyone else, no matter who they are. Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you're putting me on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't like to hear my accent.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have a very beautiful accent. What's wrong with the accent? I have an accent, too. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'd better not.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You want to say thank you or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I thank all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you very much. I appreciate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Ike, for sharing with us, and Miriam, for you being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
